metadata.career_relevance: Before deploying optimizations to production, you validate them with load testing. Zerodha runs Gatling tests before every market-open season. After JVM + DB + cache tuning: does the service actually meet p99 < 200ms at 500 req/s? Gatling tells you in 2 minutes, not after a production incident.

hook.real_world_problem:
Razorpay's payment service was optimized: JVM tuned, indexes added, cache added. Does it actually meet SLOs?

```
Gatling load test: 5-minute run, 500 users, ramp 30s:

BEFORE optimization:
  p50: 180ms  p95: 1,200ms  p99: 4,800ms  ← SLO VIOLATION!
  Error rate: 2.3%
  Requests/sec: 247 (throughput limited)

AFTER optimization (JVM + indexes + cache):
  p50: 45ms   p95: 120ms    p99: 195ms    ← SLO MET!
  Error rate: 0.02%
  Requests/sec: 498 (2× throughput)

Conclusion: all optimizations proven to work under real load.
```
hook.what_you_will_build: A Gatling simulation for Razorpay's payment API: ramp-up phase, sustained load, SLO assertions on p99 latency and error rate.

visual_aid.description: Gatling report page: top chart shows users over time (ramp from 0 to 100 over 30s, then constant 100). Bottom-left: response time percentiles over time (p50=45ms, p95=120ms, p99=195ms, all stable). Bottom-right: requests per second (steady 498 req/s). Summary table: total requests=14,940, KO=3 (0.02%), min=12ms, mean=51ms, p95=120ms, p99=195ms, max=312ms. All assertions: PASS (green checkmarks).
visual_aid.alt_text: Gatling load test report showing response time percentiles and throughput over test duration

worked_example.scenario: Load test Razorpay payment service after all M22 optimizations. Verify SLOs under 500 req/s.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. Gatling simulation: ramp to 500 users, 2-minute sustain
2. Assertions: p99 < 200ms, error rate < 0.1%, throughput > 450 req/s
3. Feeder: CSV with merchant IDs and amounts
4. Run and interpret the report

step_by_step_solution.details:
```
gatling/results/paymentload-*/index.html → open in browser
Look for: p99 latency trend (should stay flat during sustained load)
Red requests: any failures → investigate errors in request-level report
Assertions: PASS = SLOs met
```

worked_example.final_code:
# Run: mvn gatling:test
# Report: target/gatling/paymentloadsimulation-*/index.html
# CI: add to GitHub Actions (run on staging before prod deploy)
worked_example.expected_output:
================================================================================
Assertions:
  Global: 99th percentile of response time is less than 200 - OK
  Global: 95th percentile of response time is less than 120 - OK
  Global: percentage of successful requests is greater than 99.9 - OK
  Global: mean requests/sec is greater than 45 - OK

Simulation PaymentLoadSimulation completed in 151 seconds
things_to_notice: Response time assertions: p99.lt(200) means the simulation FAILS if p99 exceeds 200ms. This is how you enforce SLOs in CI — failing Gatling = blocking deployment.

injectOpen vs injectClosed: Open = arrival rate (users arrive at a rate, regardless of current active users — models real internet traffic). Closed = concurrent users (maintain exactly N active users at all times — models limited user pools). For APIs: usually injectOpen.
Feeder: CSV file provides test data variability. Without feeder: every request uses the same merchant ID — DB may cache it specifically, making results unrealistically good.
warmup phase (atOnceUsers(10) before ramp): ensures JIT has compiled hot paths before measuring performance. Without warmup: first requests are slower (C1 JIT, not C2) — inflates p99.

industry_examples.use_case: Every Friday: Gatling test on staging. Profile: ramp to 1000 users (market open surge), sustain 10 minutes. Assertions: order execution p99 < 500ms, throughput > 800 req/s. If assertions fail: investigation before next trading week. Prevents 9:15am production incidents.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you validate performance before deploying?' → Gatling load test on staging. Scenario: ramp to expected peak load, sustain for 5-10 minutes. Assertions: p99 < SLO, error rate < 0.1%, throughput > minimum. Fail the CI pipeline if assertions fail. Run after every significant change.
applications.real_world_workflow: Load testing workflow: (1) write Gatling simulation, (2) run on staging with production-like data, (3) check assertions, (4) if fail: profile with async-profiler or Zipkin traces, (5) fix bottleneck, (6) re-run Gatling until assertions pass.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using the same merchant ID for all load test requests. DB caches this specific record. Results are unrealistically fast (cache hit every time). Real production: random merchant IDs.
common_gaps.remediation: Use Gatling feeder: CSV with 1000 merchant IDs, random selection. This ensures cache pressure is realistic (mix of hits and misses).
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Load test p50 = 1ms (suspiciously fast); production p50 = 45ms. Root cause: test used cached data; production has random access patterns

key_takeaways: Gatling: validates optimizations under real load before production
injectOpen: models real traffic (arrival rate). injectClosed: models limited concurrency.
Assertions: p99.lt(200), successRate.gt(99.9) — fails CI if violated
Feeder: CSV/JSON test data for realistic cache miss patterns
Warmup phase (atOnceUsers): JIT compilation before measuring
Gatling report: response time percentile charts + throughput over time
Run in CI: blocking deployment if performance regresses

self_check_questions.question: Your load test passes with p99 = 180ms at 100 users. How do you know if it will still pass at 500 users?
self_check_questions.answer: You don't — 100 users might be within comfortable headroom. Scale test: run multiple Gatling simulations with increasing users (100, 200, 300, 400, 500) and record p99 at each level. This is a 'capacity test' or 'stress test'. Look for: the point where p99 exceeds SLO (500ms). That's your concurrency limit. Also test 'spike test': jump from 0 to 500 users instantly — does the system degrade gracefully (circuit breakers fire) or fail catastrophically? For trading services: test market-open surge (rampUsers(500).during(10)) — 500 users arriving in 10 seconds.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 22: Performance & Optimization is complete! You've mastered JVM tuning (T22.1), database query optimization (T22.2), connection pool sizing (T22.3), two-level caching (T22.4), and load testing (T22.5). Module 23 covers Security — JWT authentication, OAuth2, secrets management, and input validation.
